description: A ranking is always defined relative to one or more criteria that determine the order. For currencies
weight: common criteria include trading turnover

description: The superlative form of relevance
weight: used to identify the items deemed most important within a given set according to chosen criteria.

description: Trading turnover is the total volume of trades executed in a market over a specified period. For foreign exchange
weight: it is typically measured in USD-equivalent value.

description: Daily turnover aggregates all trades executed within a single trading day.
Weekly turnover aggregates daily turnover figures across a calendar week.
Yearly turnover aggregates trading activity across a calendar or fiscal year
weight: providing a long-term view of market activity.

description: A ranking that uses daily turnover as the ordering criterion to compare currencies
weight: exchanges

description: A ranking that uses weekly turnover as the ordering criterion.
A ranking that uses yearly turnover as the ordering criterion. This is typically considered the most stable and relevant criterion for ranking currencies.
Yearly turnover ranking is treated here as the canonical 'most relevant' ranking for currencies
weight: as it smooths out short-term volatility.

description: Weekly turnover ranking provides a mid-term view of currency relevance
weight: balancing immediacy with stability.

description: Daily turnover ranking gives the most immediate view of currency relevance but is more volatile than longer-term rankings.

triple_name: currency has a primary ranked by yearly turnover
description: Every currency in this dataset is primarily ordered by its yearly turnover
weight: the default ranking criterion.

triple_name: currency can have ranked by weekly turnover
description: Currencies may also be ranked by weekly turnover where a shorter-term view is needed.

triple_name: currency can have ranked by daily turnover
description: Currencies may also be ranked by daily turnover
weight: the most volatile of the supported ranking criteria.

description: Turnover figures are often reported as averages (typically arithmetic means) over a defined period to give a representative value rather than a one-off snapshot.
Daily average turnover expresses turnover as a per-day mean
weight: calculated by dividing total turnover in a period by the number of trading days.

description: Monthly average turnover expresses turnover as a per-month mean over a longer reporting horizon.
Yearly average turnover expresses turnover as a per-year mean
weight: typically derived from monthly averages.

description: Indicates that a monetary value is expressed in United States dollars
weight: the standard reporting unit for international foreign exchange statistics.

description: A reporting convention used in BIS foreign exchange surveys in which transactions are adjusted to remove double-counting both between local reporting dealers and between cross-border reporting dealers
weight: yielding a true measure of global activity.

description: The ¥ symbol is used to denote the Japanese yen
weight: although the same glyph is also used for the Chinese yuan.

description: The dollar sign $ is most commonly used as the symbol for the United States dollar
weight: although it is also used for many other dollar-denominated currencies.

description: An exchange rate is the rate at which one currency will be exchanged for another. It is also regarded as the value of one country's currency in relation to another currency.
The amount of money being converted from the source currency
weight: expressed in units of that source currency.

description: The amount of money produced by the conversion
weight: expressed in units of the target currency.

description: The currency from which an amount is converted in a currency-conversion operation.
The currency into which an amount is converted in a currency-conversion operation.
The process of changing an amount of money from one currency into the equivalent amount in another currency at the prevailing exchange rate.
A cross rate is an exchange rate between two currencies
weight: neither of which is the official currency of the country in which the quote is given. More generally
triple_condition_id: it is a rate derived from each currency's rate against a common third currency such as the US dollar.

description: The bid price is the highest price a buyer (typically a market maker) is willing to pay for a currency at a given moment.
The ask price (also called the offer price) is the lowest price a seller (typically a market maker) is willing to accept for a currency at a given moment.

The bid-ask spread is the difference between the ask price and the bid price for a given currency or other asset. It represents the implicit transaction cost of trading and is a key indicator of market liquidity.
The bid-ask spread expressed as a percentage of the ask price
weight: allowing direct comparison of spreads across different currencies and price levels.

description: The movement in an exchange rate between two points in time
weight: expressed either in absolute terms or as a percentage.

description: The earlier of two exchange rate observations
weight: used as the baseline in computing a rate change.

description: The more recent of two exchange rate observations
weight: compared against the old rate to compute a rate change.

description: A change expressed as a fraction of the original value times 100. Standard formula: (new − old) / old × 100.

A forward exchange rate is the rate at which a bank agrees today to exchange one currency for another at a specified future date. Forward rates are derived from spot rates and interest rate differentials via the covered interest parity relationship.
The spot rate is the current exchange rate at which a currency pair can be bought or sold for immediate delivery (typically T+2 settlement).
The interest rate is the proportion of a loan or deposit that is charged or paid as interest
weight: typically expressed as an annual percentage. Central bank policy rates are key drivers of currency valuations and forward rates.

description: The interest rate applicable to the base currency of a currency pair. Higher base-currency interest rates relative to the quote currency typically lead to forward rates below spot.
The interest rate applicable to the quote currency of a currency pair. Higher quote-currency interest rates relative to the base currency typically lead to forward rates above spot.
